content:
3 min read
Just now

--

JavaScript running in browsers and Node.js comes with an event loop implementation. They are similar because they both schedule and execute asynchronous tasks but their implementations differ because they are designed for different environments. Understanding the event loop is very important as it handles the scheduling of your application code. The event loop is responsible for scheduling and executing callbacks for asynchronous operations.
Event loop phases
The event loop has several different phases. Each of these phases maintains a queue of callbacks that need to be executed. Callbacks are assigned to different phases depending on how they are used by the application.

• Timers ➖ Callbacks scheduled using setTimeout() and setInterval() are executed in this phase.

• Pending ➖ Special system-level callbacks are executed in this phase, such as when a net.Socket TCP socket throws an ECONNREFUSED error.

• Poll ➖ This phase executes I/O-related callbacks and retrieves new I/O events. If there is no immediate work to do, Node.js may wait here for incoming I/O events.

• Check ➖ Callbacks scheduled using setImmediate() are executed in this phase.

• Close ➖ Callbacks associated with close events, such as socket.on('close'), are executed in this phase.

• There are also two special microtask queues that can have callbacks added to them while a phase is running. The first microtask queue handles callbacks that have been registered using process.nextTick(). The second microtask queue handles Promise callbacks such as .then(), .catch(), and .finally(). Callbacks in the microtask queues take priority over callbacks in the event loop phase queues and callbacks in the process.nextTick() queue run before callbacks in the Promise microtask queue.
When the application starts, the main JavaScript code executes first. After that, the event loop begins processing its phases one at a time. Node.js adds callbacks to the appropriate queues while the application runs. When the event loop reaches a phase, it executes the callbacks in that phase’s queue. Once all the callbacks in that phase have been executed, the event loop moves to the next phase. If there is no immediate work to do but Node.js is waiting for I/O operations to complete, it may remain in the Poll phase until new events arrive.
Example
const fs = require('fs');
setImmediate(() => console.log(1));
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(2));
process.nextTick(() => console.log(3));
fs.readFile(__filename, () => {
console.log(4);
setTimeout(() => console.log(5));
setImmediate(() => console.log(6));
process.nextTick(() => console.log(7));
});
console.log(8);Output — 8, 3, 2, 1, 4, 7, 6, 5
Execution

• Synchronous code → 8

• Microtasks → process.nextTick() → 3, then Promise → 2

• Check phase → setImmediate() → 1

• Poll phase → fs.readFile() callback → 4

• Microtasks after Poll callback → 7

• Check phase → 6

• Next Event Loop iteration (Timers) → 5
why does setImmediate() execute before setTimeout() inside an I/O callback?

• The fs.readFile() callback is executed in the Poll phase.

• Inside that callback, both setTimeout() and setImmediate() are scheduled.

• After the Poll phase, the Event Loop immediately enters the Check phase, where setImmediate() callbacks are executed.

• setTimeout() is placed in the Timers queue and waits for the next iteration of the Event Loop.

• Therefore, setImmediate() runs before setTimeout().
Notice how microtasks drain completely after every phase — that’s why process.nextTick and Promise callbacks always jump ahead of the phase queues.
